captions.text: At 11:46 PM, during a brownout in my grandmother’s house, my cousin Carlo sat straight up in bed and whispered:
“Mom… there’s someone underneath.”
His mother thought he meant under the bed.
So she got a candle, knelt down, and looked.
There was nothing there.
Just dust. One missing slipper.
And a trail of wet soil leading from Carlo’s bed to the wall.
The strange part was, nobody had gone outside.
But at the bottom of the wall, right beside Carlo’s bed, someone had written three tiny words in mud:
“He didn’t ask.”
That afternoon, Carlo had been playing behind the house with the other kids.
There was an old santol tree there.
Under it was a small mound of dirt that everyone avoided, although no one ever explained why.
When the ball rolled beside it, the younger kids froze.
Carlo laughed.
He said, 'It’s just dirt.'
Then
he picked up the ball.
Nobody saw what happened next clearly.
One cousin said Carlo stepped on the mound.
Another said he kicked something.
But everyone agreed on one thing.
Right after Carlo touched it, the rain stopped for exactly ten seconds.
And from under the santol tree, they heard an old man cough.
That night, Carlo developed a fever.
His
right foot turned red and swollen.
His grandmother didn’t ask what happened.
She
just looked at his foot, then at the muddy writing on the wall, and said: “Why
did you bring him inside?”
No one slept after that.
Because
every few minutes, something would tap from inside the wall.
Three
soft taps. Then
a pause. Then
Carlo’s voice would answer, even though Carlo was asleep: “I’m
here.” Near
sunrise, his mother finally found the missing slipper.
It
was inside Carlo’s pillowcase. Filled
with black soil. And
tucked inside it was a tiny piece of paper, folded three times.
His
grandmother opened it, read it once, and started crying.
Carlo’s
mother asked what it said.
But
his grandmother only shook her head and whispered: “He
isn’t the one they’re looking for.” That
was when the crib began to rock.
